g.data-narration: Welcome to Model-based Learning! This powerful reinforcement learning approach uses environment models to make intelligent predictions.
Model-based learning is a reinforcement learning approach where the agent learns a model of the environment to predict future states and rewards.
g.text: A type of reinforcement learning that uses a model of the environment
to make predictions about future states and rewards before taking actions.

g.data-narration: The agent observes states, learns a model from experience, then uses that model to plan optimal actions through value or policy iteration.
Model-based learning has two essential components: model learning where the agent learns environment dynamics, and planning where it uses the model to decide actions.
g.text: Uses model to compute optimal policy

g.data-narration: There are two main approaches to model learning: table-based methods store all state-action transitions, while function approximation learns a generalizable function.
Planning uses the learned model through value iteration to compute value functions, or policy iteration to directly optimize the policy.
Three main types exist: model-based value iteration computes values using the model, policy iteration optimizes policy directly, and MBRL combines both approaches.
Model-based learning is more sample-efficient because the agent can simulate many trajectories using the learned model without interacting with the real environment.
Model-based learning enables better exploration because agents can predict outcomes of unexplored actions using the model before trying them in reality.
Model-based learning has higher computational complexity because the agent must learn the model, store it, and perform planning computations at each step.
Model bias occurs when the learned model is inaccurate, leading to suboptimal decisions since the agent trusts its predictions which may not reflect reality.
In robotics, model-based learning enables robots to learn environment models and plan movements in simulation before executing in the real world safely.
g.text: Plan in simulation → Execute in reality

g.data-narration: In finance, model-based learning helps predict market movements and optimize investment portfolios by learning models of market behavior from historical data.
Model-based methods learn environment models and plan, while model-free methods like Q-learning directly learn action values from experience without a model.
g.text: Examples: Dyna-Q, MBVE,蒙特卡洛树搜索

g.data-narration: Key formulas include the transition probability T(s,a), reward function R(s,a), and the Bellman equations for value functions and optimal policies.
In summary, model-based learning uses environment models for sample-efficient planning but faces challenges of computational complexity and model bias.
